steps.title: Identify the clause structure around the blank
steps.content: The sentence reads: "Jetties can sometimes have the opposite [blank] obstructing the natural flow of sand along the shore can lead to increased erosion in some areas." The left side ("Jetties can sometimes have the opposite effect") is a complete independent clause. The right side ("obstructing…areas") is also a complete independent clause. The blank must fill in the noun effect plus punctuation that connects the word though and joins the two independent clauses correctly.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
The rule: two independent clauses require a semicolon (or period) between them; a comma alone creates a comma splice. The adverb though must be set off by a comma from the clause it modifies:
  • Choice A — 'effect, though;': "effect" completes the first clause; the comma correctly sets off though as a supplementary adverb; the semicolon then properly separates the two independent clauses. ✓
  • Choice B — 'effect, though': No semicolon to separate the two independent clauses — this fuses them without proper boundary punctuation. ✗
  • Choice C — 'effect; though': A semicolon before though makes "though obstructing…areas" a dependent clause after the semicolon, which violates the rule that a semicolon must be followed by an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice D — 'effect, though,': Two commas set off though as an interrupter, but no boundary separates the two independent clauses — comma splice. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
